data.title: There Seems No Net Benefit To A Shell-BP Merger For Shell Or Its Shareholders
data.description:
Investment Thesis

News that Shell (NYSE:SHEL) is exploring the merits of a merger with BP (NYSE:BP) has the market excited about the potential creation of a global oil patch giant. It is most certainly true that the two companies merged into one, with a combined market cap of about $270 billion, would make it into an oil sector giant, surpassing Chevron (CVX) in terms of market cap. My take on the potential merger is that it would dilute Shell's stronger segments in terms of their overall effect on profitability. Both companies share some of the same weaknesses, but BP's profits are far more deeply affected, with fewer bright spots to help cover those weaknesses. It remains to be seen what conclusions Shell's leadership will come to regarding a possible merger with BP. From an investor's point of view, I do not see it as an opportunity for Shell, but perhaps a decent chance for BP investors to see a bump in share prices. At this point, it appears that Shell's leadership does not see the merger as an ideal opportunity; therefore, it is unlikely to happen, which makes Shell still a buy, while BP is a hold, as I illustrated in past articles.

In My Past Coverage of Both Companies, I Highlighted That Shell's Most Attractive Segment is Its LNG Business, While BP Has Fewer Bright Spots

I last covered Shell in February, when I highlighted its strong position in the global LNG market, where I see a great deal of opportunity, given Europe's growing reliance on LNG imports, as well as other factors. BP does not have any such bright spots to point to, as a counterbalance to its weak spots, such as declining upstream production, as I pointed out in an article toward the end of 2024. A lack of upstream reserves, even as BP is eagerly announcing a pivot away from green energy and back into the oil & gas business, calls into question its ability to successfully change course. As such, I had Shell as a buy and BP as a hold in my past articles that covered these companies, something that goes into my reasoning behind my evaluation of a possible Shell-BP merger.

Shell's Superior Financial Performance Relative to BP is Due in Large Part to Its Globally Dominant LNG Segment

With both companies having reported Q1 results in the past two weeks, we can see a significant difference in performance between the two companies, with Shell's superior position in LNG and perhaps a more solid upstream situation being the main differentiating factors.

Shell reported adjusted net earnings of $5.58 billion for the first quarter of the year. Over $4.8 billion can be attributed to the LNG and upstream segments.

BP saw a net profit of $684 million in Q1, a massive improvement on the Q4 2024 loss of almost $2 billion. The quarter-on-quarter improvement makes for a positive headline, but it also hides BP's continued weak performance by other financial metrics. For instance, Shell's profit margin was about 8% on revenues of just over $70 billion. BP brought in almost $47 billion in revenues for the same period, meaning that it achieved a profit margin of only 1.5%.

In terms of their respective upstream performance situations, BP saw a decline in upstream output of nearly 6% compared with the same period from a year ago, while costs per unit produced increased by 5.6% for the same period.

Shell, by comparison, achieved upstream production of 1.872 mb/d in oil equivalent in Q1, 2024 compared with 1.855 mb/d for the latest quarter, meaning that its upstream production is currently more or less stable.

One final aspect of the latest financial results that I want to touch on is the debt and share issuance situation of both prospective merger candidates. Shell has a total of $65.12 billion in long-term debt, which is a slight decline from the end of last year.

Shell's debt amounts to about 33% of its current market cap.

BP's debt is $53.8 billion, which comes out to about 73% of its current market cap. The worrying part is that BP's debt load is on a path of steady & robust growth.

Both Shell & BP Suffer From a Lack of Robust Upstream Oil & Gas Reserves

The issue of global oil & gas reserves has been debated in a slightly less matter-of-fact and more ideological manner in the past decade or so. The peak oil supply crowd that gained some prominence at the start of the century took a real beating when the US shale boom flooded the global oil & gas markets. On top of it, the peak oil demand theory gave rise to the idea that oil & gas reserves that oil companies are sitting on can be considered as good as stranded assets, since most of them will never be produced, thanks to the EV revolution.

Total yearly global conventional oil & gas production is about 40-45 billion barrels of oil equivalent, depending on how one chooses to define conventional resources. As the chart shows, even in the best years in the past decade, about half as much was discovered globally as was produced. 2023 was a particularly bad year, with only 5 billion barrels of oil equivalent being discovered. The decade so far has yielded only about 10 billion barrels of oil & gas in discoveries on average every year, covering only about a quarter of global conventional oil & gas production.

The implications for both Shell & BP, two companies that initially embraced the peak oil demand theory, and thus invested heavily in transitioning to green energy, are that they neglected to shore up their upstream reserves. Now that they are realizing that they cannot make money in the green energy segment, and global oil & gas demand did not peak, both are looking to pivot back toward the oil sector. Neither company has a solid upstream situation when it comes to reserves.

Shell has total proven oil and gas reserves of 9.62 billion barrels of oil equivalent. Although reserves have been falling, they have not been at a dramatic pace. Shell's reserves seem to have supported steady production levels for the past few years.

BP's total upstream production declined from 2.38 mb/d in Q1 2024 to 2.24 mb/d in the first quarter of this year. BP has 3.7 billion barrels of oil in reserves, which at current production rates would be enough for roughly a decade, similar to its natural gas situation.

Summing up the situation for both Shell & BP, they are both short on reserves, even as both are trying to reorient themselves toward oil & gas. For comparison, my largest oil major holding by far in my stock portfolio, Suncor (SU) has roughly three decades worth of reserves at its current production rate. It is the main reason why I prefer it, as well as Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), over other oil producers. Oil majors need to have a robust upstream business if they are to be true oil majors. The lack of conventional oil & gas discoveries across the planet makes the lack of reserves that both companies face problematic because they are unlikely to be able to explore their way out of the predicament or to buy their way into reserves discovered by others.

While there is no shift in the general public consensus on this, I feel that peak oil supply is more likely to occur in the next few years than peak oil demand, especially given the growing signs and even warnings of US shale peaking perhaps as soon as this year. The only thing that temporarily tends to dampen global oil demand is an economic slowdown. The IMF recently revised global economic growth prospects for this year and next. Even so, global oil demand is still rising, even if at a slow pace, while cracks in supply growth are appearing, with the EIA steadily cutting its US crude oil output forecast for the past few months. A recent report suggests that we may see the first year-over-year US oil production decline as soon as next year. Companies with a strong portfolio of upstream resources that can be profitably extracted are preferable to those that do not have a lot of upstream resources. Shell is an exception, given its strong LNG segment, which currently makes up for its weak upstream position, due to global economic and geopolitical realities.

Investment Implications

Shell & BP Do Not Complement Each Other Well. Squeezing Out Synergies Would Be the Only Long-term Benefit

Both companies face a lack of upstream reserves, even as global supply/demand prospects for oil and gas are increasingly in need of re-evaluation, with holes appearing in the peak oil demand narrative. With this perspective in mind, upstream resources are the main determinant of value, and Shell would pay heavily for BP's upstream resources that come with significant non-performing assets, as well as outsized financial liabilities relative to Shell's. The bulk of BP's positive financial results stem from its upstream segment, which, based on its current upstream asset base, is shrinking in output.

The one longer-term benefit of a Shell-BP merger would be the customary reduction in operational redundancies. These range from administrative costs to cutting R&D spending, where both companies might be currently working on similar solutions for the same challenges. A sale of certain assets would also probably occur, which can lead to a cut in debt, or it could go toward investments in more lucrative directions. I am not sure that any of these potential gains would help Shell to overcome the downsides I cited in acquiring BP.

Risks to My Thesis

My current position is to maintain Shell as a buy and BP as a hold, based on my positive outlook for oil prices, as well as LNG, while I am assuming that Shell's leadership will also see the idea of a merger with BP as a net negative, which is why I do not believe that a merger will occur. If I am wrong, my current position in Shell shares might be exposed to the market discounting it, while BP shares will see a boost in the pre-merger period. In other words, BP shares would be the asset to buy & hold, not Shell stock.

Shell is Unlikely to Go for the Temporary Glory Lap of Becoming a Giant Among Oil Majors at the Expense of Longer-term Downsides

A recent statement by Shell's CEO puts to rest any speculation that Shell may be actively seeking to merge with BP in the near future. "I've said in the past we want to be value hunters. Today, value hunting, in my view, is buying back more Shell,". It is not an outright rejection of the idea but an indication that any such talk may be premature. A study of a subject does not necessarily indicate a serious consideration to act, but rather just an initial probe of a possibility, which often is destined to lead to nothing much at all, once the pros and cons are weighed.

Shell has several vulnerabilities, including many of the same ones that BP is struggling with, that make the latter a prospective takeover target. As such, I am sure that Shell's leadership will not consider a merger with BP to be an enticing opportunity. It would dilute its strong LNG position that is currently helping to shore up its profitability and other stronger aspects of its business. While I intend to keep an eye out for any further signs that Shell might be taking the idea of a BP merger seriously, at which point I may have to reconsider holding on to its shares, for now, it looks like an unlikely scenario.

BP continues to be a hold, worth keeping an eye on, in part because it might eventually become a takeover target. It might not be Shell, but at some point, some company might go for it. There is also the possibility that it might get its Russian operations back, depending on how and when the Ukraine war plays out and eventually ends, with all parties involved settling on a post-war reality. BP took a major hit from losing those upstream operations, where it had an almost 20% stake in Rosneft. It would see a major bump to its value if a favorable outcome arises once the war ends. There are scenarios where BP stock might become a good investment opportunity, but for now, it would be highly risky to bet on any of those scenarios. BP stock, therefore, remains a hold, while Shell remains a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Uncertainty Over (Trade) Policy Will Cut Hiring And Investment, Say Business Execs
data.description:
By Jose Maria Barrero, Nick Bloom, Steven J. Davis, Kevin Foster, Aaron Jalca, Brent Meyer

Chair Powell, in his post-FOMC meeting press conference last week, noted that "Surveys of households and businesses... report a sharp decline in sentiment and elevated uncertainty about the economic outlook, largely reflecting trade policy concerns. It remains to be seen how these developments might affect future spending and investment."

Indeed, uncertainty over trade policy has spiked to historic levels in recent months across a variety of measures. Going back to that press conference to hear from Chair Powell again, "There's so much uncertainty. If you talk to businesses, or market participants, or forecasters, everyone is just waiting to see how developments play out..." It's precisely this waiting to see how "developments play out" that can lead businesses, en masse, to pull back on capital investment and hiring.

To assess this concern, we put special questions to nearly 1,000 business executives in the latest Survey of Business Uncertainty, which was fielded from April 14 to 25. We first asked, "How has uncertainty about tariffs, taxes, government spending, monetary policy, or regulation affected your firm's plans for hiring [investment] over the next 6 months?"

Forty percent of the executives plan to scale back hiring at their firms due to policy uncertainty, while 45 percent anticipate pulling back on capital investment for the same reason. Only a small percentage plan to ramp up hiring and investment in response to policy uncertainty (see the chart below).

Download data

Manufacturing firms, as well as those operating in the trade and transportation sectors, reported a somewhat more broad-based pullback of investment in particular. Just over 50 percent of durable and nondurable manufacturers and slightly less than half of all retail trade, wholesale trade, and transportation firms plan to scale back investment over the near term.

We also investigated which sources of policy uncertainties are weighing most heavily on the minds of execs. If a respondent indicated either pulling back or ramping up hiring or investment due to policy uncertainty, we asked, "What is your firm's top concern with respect to uncertainty affecting your firm's hiring [investment] plans over the next 6 months?"

Here, tariffs are overwhelmingly the top source of uncertainty (see the chart below). Monetary policy ranks a distant second, followed by government spending and regulation. Perhaps unsurprisingly, manufacturing firms and those in the trade and transportation industries also more frequently cited tariffs as their top source of policy uncertainty.

Download data

Finally, we asked business executives how much policy uncertainty would lead them to alter investment and hiring plans at their firms. Averaging over all survey responses, businesses plan to slow hiring by 13 percent and scale back investment by 16 percent during the next six months due to policy uncertainty.

For executives anticipating hiring cuts, a large group of firms sits at minus 10 percent, with a long left tail. More than 5 percent of firms plan to cut hiring by at least 90 percent - essentially imposing a hiring freeze - until key uncertainties resolve. The picture is very similar for near-term investment plans (see the chart below).

Download data

Across the firm-size dimension, the survey results noted modest variation in hiring and investment plans by firm size and industry ranging from minus 10 percent to minus 17 percent. Across industries, the mean causal impact of policy uncertainty on hiring was minus 11 percent to minus 17 percent, with the largest impact again observed on manufacturing and trade and transportation industries.

For investment, the pattern is similar with a mean response range of minus 13 percent to minus 18 percent across the firm-size dimension and minus 12 percent to minus 24 percent across broad industries.

To add some context, the magnitude of these declines (at least in capital investment) are similar to what executives told us about the restraint induced by key uncertainties back in October 2020, during the first year of the pandemic.

And are much more severe than they anticipated at the onset of the war between Russia and Ukraine. These earlier results were borne out in the pattern of real business fixed investment during the late 2020–22 period, which was marked by tepid (at best) equipment and software spending and a sustained decline in structures investment.

To sum up what the survey results tell us, heightened policy uncertainty (particularly over tariffs) is leading a large share of firms to scale back near-term hiring and investment.

On average, the impact of policy uncertainty is lowering hiring and investment by 13 percent and 16 percent, respectively. To paraphrase Chair Powell, businesses are in wait-and-see mode at the moment. Should uncertainty over trade policy continue to go unresolved, its dampening effect will continue to weigh on the near-term growth trajectory of the US economy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The American Standard Of Living Is Showing Cracks
data.description:
The majority of Americans are still positive about their standard of living, but rising home prices and the lack of retirement savings are increasingly putting the American dream out of reach.

The Take

➤ Two-thirds of Americans believe they have or will have a higher standard of living than their parents.

➤ Americans are split evenly as to whether a college education is the key to future success.

➤ Nearly half of renters in the US believe they will never own a home.

➤ Most Americans under 35 years of age believe the value of their home will be a primary source of retirement income.

➤ Only 55% of Americans believe they are saving adequately for retirement.

The recently completed S&P Global Market Intelligence Kagan first-quarter 2025 US Consumer Insights survey asked respondents if they agreed or disagreed with a series of statements related to quality of life and one’s standard of living. For instance, the survey found that nearly two-thirds (63%) of internet adults believe they have or will have a higher standard of living than their parents. This generally positive outlook spans all age groups, as well as race/ethnicity, population center size, and education level. The main differentiator was household income, where approximately half (47%) of adults in low-income households earning less than $50,000 disagreed with the statement; double (23%) that of the highest-income households earning $150,000 or more.

Consumers were equally divided when responding to the statement, "a college education has been or will be the key to my being successful." Age was a dependent variable, with 71% of young adults ages 18-24 agreeing, while less than half (43%) of adults ages 65 and older responded in kind. It was unsurprising that two-thirds (66%) of those with a college degree agreed with the statement and 69% of those without a college degree disagreed. This sentiment was also apparent when segmenting by household income. Over one-third (35%) of adults with an annual household income of $150,000 or more strongly agreed, compared to only 17% of those with a household income of less than $50,000 annually.

Home ownership has traditionally been a major factor in defining the American standard of living. However, rising prices now place home ownership out of reach for many. The survey found that nearly half (47%) of those currently renting do not believe they will someday own a house. Eight out of 10 adults with a household income of $100,000 or more agreed that owning a home was in their future, compared to about half of renters earning less than $100,000. Yet, the dream of home ownership remains strong at about 75% among young adults under 35 years of age. The survey data shows that with age, however, the prospect of switching from renter to homeowner drops precipitously.

Among current homeowners, 63% disagreed with the statement “the value of my house will be my primary source of retirement income.” The data shows that in retirement, low-income homeowners (less than $50,000 per year) believe they will rely on home value more than those with higher incomes, but the variance is not substantial. However, the survey results highlight that the majority of younger adults (under 35 years of age) believe they will be relying on the value of their home in retirement. In comparison, approximately two-thirds of adults 45 and older said they have or will have other sources of retirement income.

Finally, only 55% of US internet adults believe they are on track to save an adequate amount for retirement. The survey found that income is much more of a factor with regard to retirement savings than age. The majority of survey respondents, regardless of age, said they are on track to save adequately for retirement. However, 61% of low-income earners reported they are not saving adequately for retirement, compared to only 21% of the highest income earners. Men (61%) were more likely to say they are saving adequately than women (49%). Six in 10 (61%) of those with a college degree agreed, while the majority (55%) of those without a college degree indicated they were not adequately saving for retirement.

The Kagan first-quarter 2025 US Consumer Insights survey was conducted in March 2025. The survey consisted of approximately 2,500 internet adults, with a margin of error of +/-1.9 ppts at the 95% confidence level. Survey data should only be used to identify general market characteristics and directional trends

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tariffs Weigh On Sentiment Of Big 4 Private Equity Firms
data.description:
Tariff-related uncertainty appeared to weigh on the outlook of the Big Four private equity firms as they reported first-quarter earnings.

The average net positivity score for the Big Four private equity firms dipped to its lowest level in more than a year, although the decline was less pronounced than the steeper quarter-over-quarter drop in sentiment across the S&P 500, according to an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis of the language used by executives and analysts on the calls.

The Big Four — Apollo Global Management Inc. (APO), Blackstone Inc. (BX), The Carlyle Group Inc. (CG) and KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) — are the largest publicly traded alternative asset managers by assets under management.

Among peer firms, KKR recorded the most significant drop in its first-quarter sentiment score compared to the prior four-quarter average.

That was despite CFO Robert Lewin reassuring shareholders that 90% of the firm's private equity portfolio was insulated from the immediate repercussions of tariffs and that KKR was taking steps to mitigate tariff impacts on the remainder of the portfolio. Management was also evaluating the potential of tariffs to slow global growth, lead to inflation and delay interest rate cuts, Lewin said.

"We're trying to anticipate what could happen next and be in a position where we can really react to things either proactively or very quickly if things change from our base case," Lewin said.

KKR Co-CEO Scott Nuttall said the firm aimed to "keep investing when others are scared," leaning into the macroeconomic uncertainty.

"In our experience, times like this yield some very attractive investment opportunities," Nuttall said.

Data compiled May 13, 2025.

PE = private equity.

Analysis includes average total transcript sentiment net positivity scores of select PE firms including Carlyle Group Inc., Apollo Global Management Inc., KKR & Co. Inc. and Blackstone Inc.

Net positivity score is based on the ratio of positive to negative words from the Loughran & McDonald’s (LM) Sentiment Word Lists and compares that to the total number of words. Total transcript level refers to net positivity scores at the total transcript level, where the quarterly calculation refers to the date of the earnings call transcript was released and not the earnings period.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

Exit impact

The uncertain outlook complicates the exit picture, Blackstone CEO Stephen Schwarzman acknowledged on the firm's first-quarter earnings call. Globally, the value and volume of private equity exits sank to a two-year low in the first quarter.

"More volatile markets do mean we are less likely to sell in the near term, although that can change if conditions improve," Schwarzman said.

A reduction in exits broadly would slow the return of capital to the private equity firms' limited partners, adding to the strain on the private equity investment cycle. Lower levels of distributions in recent years have created liquidity issues for limited partners, restricting their ability to make new fund commitments and contributing to a three-year decline in private equity fundraising.

AUM growth

All of the Big Four firms added to their total assets under management (AUM) in the first quarter, led by Blackstone, which reported quarterly inflows of $61.6 billion. Already the largest of the four, Blackstone grew its AUM to nearly $1.17 trillion in the first quarter, up 4.6% from $1.13 trillion in the fourth quarter 2024.

Data compiled May 13, 2025.

AUM = assets under management; PE = private equity.

Fee-earning AUM is the company's assets that are under management on which the company is earning fees. Non-fee earning AUM is calculated as the difference between total AUM and fee-earning AUM.

Analysis includes assets under management and fee-earning assets under management of Carlyle Group Inc., Apollo Global Management Inc., KKR & Co. Inc. and Blackstone Inc.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

KKR led on year-over-year growth, expanding AUM 15% to $664.32 billion as of the end of the first quarter. But Apollo was projected to grow fastest over the next year, increasing its AUM 16.7% to nearly $1.3 trillion by the first quarter of 2026, according to consensus forecast data from Visible Alpha, a part of Market Intelligence.

Carlyle, by contrast, was expected to lag its peer firms, expanding AUM 6.2% to $480.8 billion by the first quarter of 2026, according to Visible Alpha.

Total return performance

The total return performance of the Big Four firms was negative between the start of the year and the end of first-quarter earnings season, with all four firms experiencing stock price declines between Jan. 1 and mid-May. The most significant drops coincided with President Donald Trump's April 2 tariff announcement.

All four also lagged the total return performance of the S&P 500 over Jan. 1–May 12.

Blackstone paid out the largest dividend in the first quarter. The firm's 93-cent dividend is projected to rise to $1.25 by the first quarter of 2026, according to Eclipse, an S&P Global dividend forecasting service.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Kingstone's Q1 Validates My Bullish Thesis Despite Lingering Risks
data.description:
Thesis

Kingstone Companies Inc. (NASDAQ:KINS) is a regional property and casualty insurer based in New York. It focuses mainly on personal lines, especially homeowners insurance, sold through a controlled group of independent agents. The pitch is disciplined underwriting and knowing its turf well enough to hold ground in a crowded and capital-intensive market.

Back in November 2024, I rated KINS a Buy following its record-setting third-quarter results under CEO Meryl Golden. At that time, I believed Golden's leadership represented a turning point, reorienting the company toward profitability and strategic clarity after a period of near-collapse.

The valuation was equally compelling: despite a threefold-plus rebound from its lows, the stock remained deeply discounted relative to historical multiples, offering what I saw as a rare mix of operational momentum and deep value. Since that call, KINS is up 15%, handily outperforming the broader market, which has been negative over the same period, validating the core of my thesis on the stock's revival.

In this analysis, I re-evaluate that view in light of Kingstone's Q1 2025 earnings, released last week, and what emerges is a nuanced picture: a company with accelerating fundamentals and credible strategic direction, but one still navigating structural challenges that could temper the pace, or durability, of its revaluation.

Strong Q1 Results

In the hothouse ecosystem of regional insurance, where capital cushions are often outgunned by macro-scale volatility, Kingstone's Q1 2025 results land with something subtler, and maybe more threatening to its larger rivals: consistency.

Posting earnings per share of $0.27, which beat analyst expectations by $0.16, and notching $43.52 million in revenue, $1.82 million above consensus, Kingstone's numbers are impressive in isolation. However, they take on more gravity when placed against the backdrop of an industry grappling with profit cyclicality and structural pressure from both above, national-scale players, and below, an ever-commodifying broker channel.

What matters more is the shape of the curve. Kingstone's sixth consecutive quarter of profitability and a 172% year-over-year surge in net income to $3.9 million, with operating income climbing to $2.4 million, suggests a new equilibrium. Even the combined ratio, sitting at 93.7%, only modestly above last year's 93.3%, implies that underwriting discipline is holding the line, even as loss ratios come under the sort of pressure that might have derailed smaller insurers just a few quarters ago.

However, Kingstone operates in the shadow of bigger beasts like The Hanover Insurance Group (THG) and Selective Insurance Group (SIGI), who, from their command posts in Massachusetts and New Jersey, respectively, extend tentacles across personal, commercial, and specialty lines with the kind of capital efficiency that only decades of scale can buy. Yet, while THG and SIGI boast diversification and reach, their national footprint can blunt their sensitivity to local dynamics. Kingstone's tightly focused strategy, primarily on homeowners and personal lines in New York, functions more like an insurgency: agile, hyper-aware of regional quirks, and unencumbered by the drag of broader exposure.

While Kingstone isn't in direct competition with giants like Marsh & McLennan (MMC) or Gallagher (AJG), it inhabits the same distribution architecture. The problem? That architecture is increasingly consolidated. The likes of Brown & Brown (BRO) and Ryan Specialty (RYAN) are power brokers who determine which insurers get access to shelf space, which in turn drives visibility, pricing power, and ultimately, survival.

Given this structural asymmetry, Kingstone's 68% increase in new business is a sign of strategic viability in a compressed channel. It shows that Kingstone has found a way to stay sticky: by investing in local agent relationships and aligning product design tightly with regional risk appetites. The 19% rise in average renewal premiums and 10% increase in core policies in force only deepen that signal.

Select Product Shift

Kingstone's move into the "Select" product line, telegraphed in a 2022 shareholder-letter, is a rebuke of the industry's default mode. By emphasizing customers with higher deductibles and better insurance scores, Kingstone is signaling a departure from underwriting as compliance, toward underwriting as a capital efficiency mechanism. The Select line's claims frequency clocks in at 1.6%, a meaningful cut from the 2.3% experienced by the legacy book. That delta represents a reallocation of exposure. And with Select policies now making up 48% of total policies in force, and a target of 60% by year-end, the company seems to be betting the farm on it.

But risk pruning alone doesn't pay the bills, particularly not in insurance, which is why its recent agreement with AmGUARD, a Berkshire Hathaway subsidiary, functions as the perfect complement to its internal recalibration. The AmGUARD deal, anticipated to pump $25 million to $35 million in new premium over twelve months starting in Q3 2025, is preemptively efficient. The policies come attached with underwriting data, and that's the real currency here. It means faster quote cycles, better targeting, and a shortcut to building relationships with higher-value producers that would otherwise take years to develop. In this sense, the AmGUARD deal represents an arbitrage on time and trust and Kingstone gets the benefit of someone else's labor, but without the decade-long slog.

Financially, the company's position is the strongest it has been in years. In eliminating its holding company debt, Kingstone saved $800,000 in annual interest expense. This move was accompanied by a $1.5 million after-tax gain from the sale of its headquarters and adjacent property, capital which is being redeployed into core operations. With a debt-free balance sheet, the company gains not only financial flexibility but also a signal advantage when courting brokers, producers, and reinsurers who view solvency as a proxy for partnership reliability.

Portfolio Optimization

The company's investment income strategy is equally well-timed. Investment income surged 36% year-over-year, rising to $2 million from $1.5 million, a gain driven by the redeployment of $16 million into fixed income instruments yielding 5.41%, a sharp pivot that capitalizes on the current interest rate environment. With an additional $44 million in lower-yielding securities maturing by 2026, Kingstone appears well-positioned to continue harvesting higher yields.

But if the left hand is allocating with precision, the right hand is scrambling to keep pace with a digital tsunami reshaping the industry from the foundation up. The transformation of the insurance ecosystem is accelerating. On one front, enterprise tech firms like Guidewire Software (GWRE) and Duck Creek Technologies are rapidly becoming the neural infrastructure of modern insurance operations. These platforms underpin the very processes: underwriting, claims, policy servicing that once defined traditional carriers. On another, consumer-facing startups like Lemonade and Root are performing a more subtle form of demolition. Through automation, machine learning, and mobile-native interfaces, they've reengineered customer expectations, redefining what responsiveness looks like and setting new thresholds for convenience.

And while Kingstone doesn't directly compete with these upstarts on volume or geography, it would be naive to think their influence is geographically constrained. Expectations seep. The AI-powered quoting engine or frictionless claims experience that a customer enjoys with Lemonade doesn't simply vanish when that same customer logs into Kingstone's portal, it becomes the new baseline. Kingstone's core operations, still anchored in a more conventional insurance model, risk falling into a value trap: improving in ways that no longer matter. Continued success with fixed income reallocation may support near-term financials, yes. But without concurrent investment in digital transformation, the insurer runs the risk of becoming operationally obsolete.

Valuation Remains Compressed

The main cause for optimism around Kingstone rests on its sharp earnings rebound. Net income hit $18.4 million in 2024 and has since climbed to $20.8 million on a trailing twelve-month basis. That's the highest level of profitability the company has seen in close to ten years. The improvement shows up across core operations.

After several years of losses, operating income turned positive at $26.8 million in 2024 and ticked up again to $28.9 million TTM.

Revenue has also grown steadily, rising from $130.2 million in 2022 to $169.9 million today. Cash flow numbers confirm the shift. Operating cash flow, which was negative $11.3 million in 2023, swung to a record $57.9 million in 2024. Levered free cash flow followed suit at $50.5 million.

Even so, the market hasn't bought in. The stock trades at a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of 12.03x, with that multiple expected to fall to 9.18x in 2025 and 7.12x in 2026. If you adjust for net cash on the balance sheet, the effective multiple slides closer to 7x. That kind of valuation is usually reserved for companies with no growth and unclear prospects. It's out of step with a business growing earnings around 30% a year.

The reason for the disconnect likely has less to do with the numbers and more to do with memory. Kingstone's past is hard to ignore, especially the period between 2023-2024. Underwriting mistakes, erratic losses and strategic misfires have left a mark on investor sentiment. So while the current fundamentals point to progress, the hangover from earlier failures, and horrific stock performance, hasn't gone away.

And to be fair, the caution isn't entirely misplaced. The Q1 2025 earnings release technically beat expectations, but it also surfaced a few issues. Most notably, the non-catastrophe loss ratio rose 0.4 points due to a 3.3-point jump in attritional fire claims. Management said the increase came from legacy products that are no longer active and framed it as a statistical outlier. Still, it was enough to remind investors that not all risks are behind them.

Cost Pressure Ahead

Other friction points further complicate the bullish case. Start with costs like inflation in construction materials, driven in part by tariffs. Management has already flagged it, warning they might have to raise rates more aggressively if materials inflation gets worse. That may preserve margins in theory, but it also raises the risk of policyholder churn, especially when rate hikes stack up.

That problem bleeds directly into another: execution risk. The AmGUARD renewal rights deal, pitched as a way to bring in up to $35 million in new premium volume, sounds promising. But it's not in the 2025 guidance for a reason. The deal's revenue potential is speculative, and even if the top-line shows up, there's still the hard part: retaining customers while adjusting pricing. That's where churn risk becomes the point of failure.

Execution concerns are compounded by a leadership vacuum. The CFO seat remains empty, and for now, the CEO and Chief Accounting Officer are pulling double duty. That might work short-term, but it's not a real solution. It leaves the company short-handed at a moment when it's making capital allocation decisions, navigating strategic pivots and trying to restore credibility.

Since I'm being skeptical, add to this the company's current geographic concentration. Kingstone is still tethered to New York, especially the Downstate region, where regulation is heavy and competition is brutal. The company says it wants to expand beyond New York by 2026, but there's no evidence that's happening yet. Until those plans materialize, investors are left with a business that's both operationally concentrated and regionally exposed.

Even with a debt-free balance sheet, capital return, for now, is still a no-show. There's been no move on dividends since 2022 [see above], no sign of buybacks, and no clear timeline for either. Management is sticking to the reinvestment script, which might make sense in the long run. In the short run, it just leaves investors waiting, again, for a payoff that hasn't arrived.

Final Verdict

I'm sticking with a Buy on Kingstone. The company has strung together several profitable quarters, is throwing off solid free cash flow, and its underwriting is getting cleaner. It looks like an actual recovery. That said, the cracks are still there: inflation pressure, too much exposure to New York, and no permanent CFO. But at this price, the market's still pricing it like none of the progress matters. For long-term investors, the gap between what the business is doing and what the stock reflects looks worth the risk

data.title: TLTW Has Delivered Great Results, But Risks Are Rising
data.description:
The last time I discussed iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy (BATS:TLTW), we were all very much in agreement that there could have been an alpha on this ETF.

And in hindsight, we can say that it really did: compared to a year ago, the yield on ultrashort has decreased, while that of the +20-year Treasuries has remained very stable, generating a profitable scenario for TLTW. And although, in general, we are still living in an economic scenario in line with this outlook, the rolling mechanism of the buy-write strategy might not align with the technical framework of the underlying, TLT.

Now I'll explain what I mean.

Quick Recap of the Latest TLTW Rating

TLTW is a passively managed buy-write ETF that tracks the Cboe TLT 2% OTM BuyWrite Index. It holds TLT and sells monthly OTM call options to generate additional income. I believe these are two fundamental pieces of information about TLTW; The benchmark has well-defined characteristics and follows a passive logic, with the strike theoretically set 2% above TLT's price at the time of the rolling. That said, I like to use this information to build positioning strategies in an averaging-down style.

Going back to the last article, I used TLTW as the basis for a defensive strategy in light of the changing macro environment and the prospect of a new political landscape. The idea was: "I'd rather optimize cash flows in the face of uncertainty, and instead of doing it with options, I'll do it with TLTW". Because while on one hand macro dynamics seemed to point toward rate cuts, the federal deficit and the political uncertainty at the time pushed toward "higher for longer" rates, and TLT moving sideways tends to generate alpha for TLTW. Comparing total returns in hindsight, the strategy made sense.

The steepening of the curve played in our favor: yields fell for short and ultrashort maturities, as well as for medium-term ones, while the impact on 20+ year maturities was minimal. This optimized TLTW's income flow relative to TLT.

A Positive Economic Context for TLTW

Using a "disconfirmation by falsification" analysis strategy, I start by asking myself: what could be a good reason to move out of TLTW and into TLT? In my view, the expectation of new rate cuts, especially if sudden, let's say "hysterical" cuts.

Now I ask: are there conditions in place for that to happen? From the Fed's perspective, in my opinion, no.

  • Recession probability: Goldman, Yardeni, JPM, and Barclays have lowered recession odds following Trump's U-turn on tariffs.

  • The unemployment rate remains at 4.2%.

  • GDP growth, after a Q1 2025 drop of 0.3%, caused by a 41% spike in imports on false expectations of a trade war, is generally forecast above 2.3% from Q2 onward.

  • As of June 18, there's a 91% probability of no cut (Fed Watch Tool), and even Powell's early-year guidance for two 25 bps cuts in 2025 is now being questioned.

  • Inflation came in below expectations, with a YoY rate still at 2.3%, above the Fed's target.

So, there's no solid (hard data) reason to expect sudden rate cuts (at most, there could be "political" cuts); thus, a balanced scenario that, in my view, supports the hypothesis of a continued sideways trend.

The Technical Risk of TLTW

The problem is that TLTW is doing a rolling operation during a moment of bearish overstretch on 20+ year Treasuries, and that's something I typically don't like. The counterintuitive part is that the downward move in TLT was caused by the very economic context that made TLTW more attractive than TLT.

TLTW, in fact, holds TLT at 100% net exposure and, with the price at $85, has calls sold at a $90 strike (as part of the rolling strategy) expiring Friday, opened around April 16-17.

Assuming a likely roll to a strike ~2% above TLT's spot price (in line with the Cboe TLT 2% OTM BuyWrite Index), the new option strike could land around $87-88. Now, just look at that on the chart, it can be concerning.

Now I simply ask: what will happen when the market starts realizing that yields are rising, prices are falling, despite a theoretically lower intrinsic risk (stable economy)? In my view, considering the lows reached, whether rates are cut or not, demand for Treasuries would return.

Conclusion

So, in my opinion, buying TLTW with TLT at price lows, at attractive yields, and in a "stable" economic scenario increases the risk of underperformance, even if there's no sign of Fed rate cuts. And while this doesn't call into question the quality of TLTW at all, I believe it justifies the HOLD rating.

Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 4% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.34%; for Investor A shares: 0.59%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 4.07% (Institutional shares) and 4.10% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s tactical relative value positioning in U.K. versus European nominal rates added value. Tactical positioning in U.S. nominal rates detracted as a surge in rate volatility moved the market against the fund’s stance.
  • The fund closed its overweight position in U.S. duration (interest rate sensitivity) in February as rates rallied. It then shifted to an underweight exposure, focusing on the front-end and “belly” of the yield curve relative to the benchmark.

Contributors

Detractors
A long position in emerging market nominal rates contributed positively as spreads tightened modestly from recent wide levels. In addition, tactical relative value positioning in U.K. versus European nominal rates added value. This positioning benefited from the underperformance of euro long-end nominal bonds compared with U.K. 30- year bonds.

Tactical positioning in U.S. nominal rates suffered amid a surge in rate volatility, which moved the market against the fund’s stance. A slight widening of credit spreads weighed on the credit strategies and inflation-linked commercial mortgage-backed securities, which resulted in a modest drag on overall performance.

Further insight

During the quarter, the fund maintained its long position in rental inflation-linked securities, which we continue to view as a robust proxy for shelter inflation. The fund closed its overweight position in U.S. duration in February as rates rallied and shifted to an underweight exposure, focused on the front-end and belly of the yield curve relative to the benchmark. The fund maintained this positioning at quarter-end, albeit in a moderately smaller size. The team gradually increased the allocation to agency mortgages as an expression of short interest rate volatility and due to relatively attractive valuations.

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% of its assets in inflation-indexed bonds of varying maturities issued by U.S. and non-U.S. governments, their agencies or instrumentalities, and U.S. and non-U.S. corporations.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. Fixed income risks include interest-rate and credit risk. Typically, when interest rates rise, there is a corresponding decline in bond values. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the bond issuer will not be able to make principal and interest payments. Principal of mortgage- or asset- backed securities normally may be prepaid at any time, reducing the yield and market value of those securities. Obligations of U.S. government agencies are supported by varying degrees of credit but generally are not backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government. Non- investment-grade debt securities (high-yield/junk bonds) may be subject to greater market fluctuations, risk of default or loss of income and principal than higher-rated securities. International investing involves risks including, but not limited to political risks, currency fluctuations, illiquidity and volatility. These risks may be heightened in emerging markets. If the index measuring inflation falls, the principal value of inflation-indexed bonds will go down and the interest payable will be reduced. Any increase in the principal amount will be considered taxable ordinary income. Repayment of the original bond principal upon maturity (adjusted for inflation) is guaranteed for U.S. Treasury inflation-indexed bonds. For bonds that do not provide a guarantee, the adjusted principal value repaid at maturity may be less than the original principal. The fund may use derivatives to hedge investments or seek enhanced returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 The Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index comprises inflation-protected public obligations of the U.S. Treasury.

Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.48%; for Investor A shares: 0.73%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of -5.50% (Institutional shares) and -5.53% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s underperformance of its benchmark was led by trending sentiment insights and macro-thematic measures, which struggled amid the changing leadership dynamics.
  • From a sector-positioning perspective, the fund remained largely neutral. However, there were slight overweight allocations to the health care and consumer staples sectors, and slight underweight exposures to the industrials and utilities sectors.

Contributors

Detractors

Certain insights provided support during the quarter. Fundamental quality measures looking at balance sheet strength and evaluating expected company default frequency helped to correctly position the fund in the health care sector. Elsewhere, sentiment insights using alternative data to capture retail preferences and credit card transactions worked well. Looking at informed investor positioning and using text-based measures to gauge broker sentiment helped to offset losses. These insights overcame sharp de-risking in early March as investors rotated out of previous momentum winners.

 The fund’s weak relative performance was mainly driven by macro-thematic insights. Measures looking at historical data to identify similarities in the current economic regime incorrectly positioned the fund against changing leadership dynamics. Other macro-thematic insights evaluating job-hiring trends proved wrong-footed. Collectively, these helped to motivate unsuccessful overweight positioning in the information technology sector. This coincided with weakness that was driven by fundamental quality insights looking at cash-flow generation and long-term asset efficiency. These measures struggled during earnings season.

Further insight

After touching all-time highs in February, global equities declined sharply to end in negative territory. U.S. market stress drove the sell-off as uncertainty about the new administration’s policy agenda simultaneously stoked recession concerns and market volatility. Combined with cooling sentiment toward the artificial intelligence (AI) opportunity, after Chinese companies released rival, low-cost AI models, market leadership rotated as elevated U.S. valuations came under pressure. U.S. small-cap stocks continued to underperform large-cap equities during the quarter. Unsurprisingly, despite concerns about a tariff-led acceleration of inflation, economic growth worries drove a sharp drop in bond yields. In contrast, international markets proved resilient as investors repositioned for the end of the U.S. exceptionalism theme.

data.title: Petrobras Q1: Higher CapEx, Weaker Oil Prices, And Fewer Dividends Ahead
data.description:
I've been bearish on Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) (NYSE:PBR.A) recently, and more specifically, since I wrote my last article on the company at the beginning of March, my pessimism has proved correct as shares have plummeted by around 9.3% since then.

Last time, the core of my bearish thesis was based on the results for 4Q24, where there was a growing blockage of the dividend thesis, the most sensitive factor for the stock's performance, as the distribution projection was no longer so attractive against a backdrop of: (1) tighter costs and expenses; (2) a much more compressed Brent price; (3) weaker E&P production; (4) refining margins operating at low efficiency levels; (4) debt increased to the highest levels in the last two years, with a net debt/EBITDA LTM adjusted of 1. 29x in 2024 vs. 0.85x in 2023, and finally (5) a structurally higher CapEx, given the current mandate of a more aggressive strategic plan.

Petrobras remains committed to honoring its policy of distributing at least 45% of its free cash flow in dividends. The problem is that, against the backdrop described above, cash flows have been weaker over the past few quarters. And the same thing happened in 1Q25—a sharp, but expected, year-over-year drop of 30.7%.

Thus, with CapEx tending to remain at higher levels, I anticipate that concern in the markets should remain regarding the investment thesis based on dividends, intensified mainly with Brent oil prices at the lowest levels since 2022, further pressuring free cash flow generation.

The more aggressive investment policy should make this point even more relevant for shareholders, with Petrobras' management team declaring that "we are stepping on the accelerator", precisely at a time of weaker Brent oil.

Oil has been very volatile since the beginning of April due to tariff announcements and uncertainty about global GDP growth, which consequently affects demand for the commodity. The tariffs in question are those announced by U.S. President Donald Trump with the countries the Americans trade with internationally, amid much uncertainty about the adoption, negotiations, and effects of the import duties.

So all in all, 1Q25 didn't bring any good news for the dividend thesis. On the contrary, it brought an increase in CapEx and no extraordinary dividends.

Although yields remain robust, their longevity at the same levels is doubtful and is a red flag for the bullish thesis.

Turning the Valves on CapEx

Recent comments from the top brass of Brazil's state-owned oil company give the slightest hint that the current moment for Petrobras is one of playing offense, rather than reaping the rewards of its solid and stable business.

In February, CEO Magda Chambriard said:

We will have a lot going on in refining and energy transition in 2025, both in investment for expansion and in refinery maintenance. We intend to invest in capacity expansion."

As Petrobras plans to invest $111 billion over the next five years, the management team also notes that "it's been a long time since this 'mid and downstream' (refining and logistics activities) has seen so much money. It's coming through, that's real".

Thus, Chambriard's management already has and should invest relatively more than the company has historically done, when it only met 70% of the CapEx forecast in the strategic plan. In general, he said, this will lead to a real impact of 35% more investment, a percentage that will be higher than that considering only refining.

In total, the strategic plan up to 2029 foresees increases of 9% in general investment and 17% in refining alone compared to the previous document, which should be more than tripled considering the effort to align forecast and realization made by the current management.

Moving on to 1Q25, investments totaled $4 billion in the quarter (vs. $3 billion in 1Q24), 67% of which went to the E&P segment. Annualizing the investment flow presented in 1Q25 would bring CapEx for the year to $16 billion vs. the investment guidance of $18 billion for 2025 as a whole—representing an achievement of 85% of the company's guidance. It's important to mention that this was one of the points of attention in relation to the past result, given the pressure of investments on the company's cash generation and, consequently, on the dividend flow going forward.

In short, the prognosis is that CapEx will get higher and higher, which in theory means that free cash flow will be impacted—at least in the short term. Although free cash flow increased by 20.4% sequentially in 1Q25, it's still 30.7% below the same period last year.

But above all, the big problem is that the return on these investments is highly questionable considering that E&P projects involve very high costs and long lead times (as well as technical and environmental risks), and also a long history of Petrobras' governance being focused on public policy interests because it is state-owned (e.g. maintaining subsidized fuels or investing in less profitable projects for political reasons), with a long history of these investments proven inefficient and yielded poor returns.

For example, between 2010 and 2024, Petrobras launched one of the largest CapEx programs in the world, investing more than $200 billion in that period, with an extensive focus on E&P and Refining. At that point, delays and cost overruns (especially in refining projects), coupled with corruption scandals and inefficient capital allocation based on political interference, have driven the company to the brink, especially with the fall in oil prices in 2014. Any similarities with the current scenario? Perhaps so.

Yield Remains Strong, but for How Long

Investors are certainly not attracted to Petrobras' investment thesis based on its plans to invest in E&P and energy transition (wind, solar, biofuels, green hydrogen). What actually makes the bullish case for PBR a no-brainer for many is its generous dividend yield.

And it is precisely for this reason that I have my reservations about the company's bullish thesis.

In the most recent earnings call, the management team highlighted the continuity of the mandatory dividend policy (45% of free cash flow). Naturally, a lower Brent scenario should reduce the absolute nominal amount to be distributed. In this way, we can expect a better balance between shareholder returns and capital preservation and discipline. In addition, extraordinary dividends should only be distributed in the event of higher-than-expected cash generation, keeping leverage levels at levels considered healthy for Petrobras.

In 1Q25, Petrobras reported the distribution of R$11.7 billion, or $2.9 billion in regular dividends only. This meant a dividend of $0.17 per share, i.e., an annualized dividend yield of 11.4% and 2.9% in the quarter. This is still arguably an extremely robust yield, but the concern is with its sustainability.

Brent oil prices in 1Q25 fell by 9% versus the same period last year. The consequence was a lower adjusted EBITDA year-over-year by ~14%, a major detractor from the poorer free cash flow in the quarter. So, naturally, with Brent remaining at current levels (US$60–65/barrel), the trend is towards an increasingly pressured free cash flow: less chance of extraordinary dividends, and a greater chance of just meeting the mandatory minimum.

In addition, Petrobras' gross debt rose to $64.5 billion in 1Q25 from $60.3 billion in 4Q24 (impact of leasing costs, classified as debt in IFRS-16 accounting), but remains within the limit of $75 billion, disclosed in the latest Strategic Plan 2025–2029, within the mandatory dividend distribution policy.

When Dividends Mask the Downside: Risks and Final Remarks

The risks to the bearish thesis are based on a clear rebound in Brent oil prices, which could increase Petrobras' profitability over the course of this year, reducing or stabilizing the annual falls in free cash flow, and of course, following with a solid distribution of dividends or possibly extraordinary dividends.

Although Petrobras trades at seemingly super cheap valuations multiples of 2.2x its forward EV/EBITDA and could mean undervaluation, bear in mind that this is just the average of the last five years, and in my view, this low multiple is more indicative of heightened political risk and the cyclical nature of commodity prices, rather than an outright bargain.

But in short, my thesis is that Petrobras is the kind of company to own because of its generous returns to its shareholders—or perhaps in the face of a massive spike in oil prices—and not because of anything else.

That said, in my view, there was no different indication in Petrobras' 1Q25 results that could alter the bearish outlook for its ADR. On the contrary, the trend is towards a more difficult macroeconomic scenario, and the company continues to step on the investment accelerator—further depressing free cash flow generation and the continuation of the thesis of dividends at robust mid-teens levels as was the case in its not-so-distant past.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 1.08% (Institutional shares) and 1.00% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Overweight positions in the industrials sector and information technology (IT) sectors, along with stock selection in the health care sector, detracted from relative performance.
  • During the quarter, higher volatility positions and overall risk were reduced. The fund added six new positions and exited four. The purchases included Waste Connections in the industrials sector, Sanofi in the health care sector, and UBS and Toronto-Dominion Bank in the financials sector. The fund’s IT exposure was lowered through reductions in the positions in Marvell Technology and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing.

Contributors

Detractors
An overweight position in the communication services sector, including in Deutsche Telekom (OTCQX:DTEGY)(OTCQX:DTEGF), boosted relative performance. The stock remained defensive amid global tariff concerns, which did not directly impact the business. German fiscal measures buoyed the benchmark DAX index, which indirectly aided the shares. Sony (SONY)(OTCPK:SNEJF) delivered strong results and earnings upgrades, especially in its gaming, music, and studio businesses. Intesa Sanpaolo (OTCPK:ISNPY)(OTCPK:IITSF) also contributed significantly, as it benefited from macroeconomic factors that led to expectations of a gradual easing in monetary policy and hopes of reduced regulation for European banks.

Overweight positioning and security selection in both the industrials and IT sectors detracted the most during the quarter. Security selection in the health care sector also weighed on relative performance. Recruit (OTCPK:RCRRF)(OTCPK:RCRUY) was the largest detractor due to fears that U.S. policy measures would impact the jobs market. Marvell (MRVL) faced concerns about artificial intelligence (AI) capital expenditure, Amazon’s custom silicon program, and Nvidia’s competitive pricing. Novo Nordisk experienced weakness from disappointing clinical trial results for its next-generation weight-loss drug CagriSema, which raised concerns about competition in the obesity drug market.

Further insight

The fund faced a challenging quarter, with significant cross currents affecting the investment regime. We were slow to recognize these changes and were not well positioned, which required a substantial portfolio rebalancing. Market performance flipped dramatically, with strength in the United States and Japan shifting to Europe and China. The dollar and U.S. bond yields fell sharply, and U.S. exceptionalism faded as fiscal austerity and tariff measures increased uncertainty. China’s AI breakthrough and Europe’s fiscal expansion further impacted the market. We expect near-term volatility to persist, though we view this environment as favorable for stock pickers. We hope to benefit from market and valuation dislocations as long-term investors focused on companies with strong earnings power.

Investment approach

Invests primarily in a diversified portfolio of equity securities of companies outside the United States with emphasis on stocks of companies fund management believes are undervalued or have good prospects for earnings growth.
